title: Deterministic Transferable Planar Dielectric Mirrors for Investigating Strong Light-Matter Coupling
authors: Atanu Patra, Subhamoy Sahoo, Johannes Düreth, Simon Betzold, Sven Höfling
abstract: Optical cavities play a central role in photonic and quantum technologies by enhancing light-matter interactions. In semiconductor microcavities, achieving high quality (Q) factors typically relies on sophisticated epitaxial growth techniques, such as molecular beam epitaxy, which offer atomic-scale precision but are costly and limited in material compatibility. For dielectric microcavities, high Q factors can be achieved using dielectric Bragg mirrors. However, conventional deposition techniques for the top mirrors, such as plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition or sputtering, can damage embedded emitters. This limitation is particularly severe for van der Waals materials, especially atomically thin semiconductors. Moreover, the conventional top-mirror deposition can cover or degrade predefined metal contacts. Recovering electrical access typically requires additional lithography and etching steps. Here, a deterministic dry-transfer approach is developed to fabricate complete dielectric microcavities using both top and bottom SiO_2/TiO_2 Bragg mirrors without post-growth lift-off processes, reaching a Q factor ~ 4x10^3. Using a WS_2 monolayer as the active medium, clear signatures of strong exciton-photon coupling are observed at both room temperature and cryogenic temperatures. These results demonstrate an efficient cavity fabrication approach that preserves the integrity of the emitter of layered materials, enabling next generation integrated photonic devices.
summary: This paper presents a new way to make high-quality optical cavities for 2D materials without damaging them. By using a 'dry-transfer' method with PDMS, the researchers can place mirrors on top of WS2 monolayers without using harsh deposition techniques. They successfully demonstrated strong light-matter coupling, where excitons and photons interact strongly, even at room temperature. This approach is much more efficient and less wasteful than traditional methods, paving the way for integrated 2D photonic devices.
main_problem: Conventional microcavity fabrication techniques like sputtering or PECVD can damage sensitive 2D materials and degrade electrical contacts, while standard DBR fabrication leads to significant material waste.
main_result: The authors developed a deterministic dry-transfer method to create high-Q dielectric microcavities that preserve the integrity of WS2 monolayers, successfully demonstrating strong exciton-photon coupling at both room and cryogenic temperatures.
method: A viscoelastic PDMS-based dry-transfer approach is used to pick up and place SiO2/TiO2 Bragg mirror fragments onto a WS2 monolayer, avoiding high-energy deposition processes.
model_or_system: The system is a planar Fabry-Perot microcavity consisting of a WS2 monolayer embedded between a top and bottom SiO2/TiO2 distributed Bragg reflector (DBR) stack.
key_observables: Cavity mode energy, Q factor, Rabi splitting, exciton energy, coupling strength (g), and angle-resolved (k-space) photoluminescence.
important_parameters: Q factor ~ 4x10^3, Rabi splitting ~ 36 meV at room temperature, and temperature range from 175 K to 275 K.
main_assumptions: The transfer process is assumed to be damage-free and the structural integrity of the 2D material is preserved.
figures_summary: Figures show the dispersion of the cavity mode, microscope images of the structure, Lorentz fits of the spectra, and the variation of k-space photoluminescence across different temperatures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the fabrication challenge, describes the deterministic dry-transfer method and DBR fabrication, presents the characterization of the cavity modes and Q factor, demonstrates strong coupling in WS2, and analyzes the temperature dependence of the coupling strength.
why_interesting: This work provides a robust experimental platform for studying cavity QED and light-matter interactions in 2D materials, which is essential for developing scalable photonic quantum technologies and exploring polaritonic physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
